perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement immediately if the other party (i) files a petition commencing a voluntary case under the Bankruptcy Code; (ii) makes a general assignment for the benefit of creditors; (iii) admits in writing its inability to pay its debts as they become due; (iv) seeks, consents to or acquiesces in the appointment of any trustee, receiver or liquidator of it or any part of its property; or (v) has commenced against it an involuntary case under the Bankruptcy Code or a proceeding under any receivership, composition, readjustment, liquidation, insolvency, dissolution or like law or statute, which case or proceeding is not dismissed or vacated within sixty (60) days.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement immediately if the other party (i) files a petition commencing a voluntary case under the Bankruptcy Code; (ii) makes a general assignment for the benefit of creditors; (iii) admits in writing its inability to pay its debts as they become due; (iv) seeks, consents to or acquiesces in the appointment of any trustee, receiver or liquidator of it or any part of its property; or (v) has commenced against it an involuntary case under the Bankruptcy Code or a proceeding under any receivership, composition, readjustment, liquidation, insolvency, dissolution or like law or statute, regardless of whether such case or proceeding is dismissed or vacated.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'sixty (60) days' dismissal clause, the contract now allows for immediate termination even if the bankruptcy or insolvency proceeding is quickly resolved. This contradicts standard bankruptcy protections afforded to debtors under federal law, which provide a window for reorganization and potential dismissal of cases.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the 60-day window for dismissal, conflicting with the automatic stay provision of the Bankruptcy Code (11 U.S.C. § 362). The automatic stay provides a debtor with temporary protection from creditors, allowing time for reorganization. Immediate termination upon filing, regardless of dismissal, undermines this protection and potentially violates the spirit of the Bankruptcy Code.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 11 U.S. Code § 362 - Automatic stay | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 11 U.S. Code § 362 - Automatic stay U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Except as provided in subsection (b) of this section, a petition filed under section 301 , 302 , or 303 of this title, or an application filed under section 5(a)(3) of the Securities Investor Protection Act of 1970 , operates as a stay, applicable to all entities, of— (1) the commencement or continuation, including the issuance or employment of process, of a judicial, administrative, or other action or proceeding against the debtor that was or could have been commenced before the commencement of the case under this title, or to recover a claim against the debtor that arose before the commencement of the case under this title; (2) the enforcement, against the debtor or against property of the estate, of a judgment obtained before the commencement of the case under this title; (3) any act to obtain possession of property of the estate or of property from the estate or to exercise control over property of the estate; (4) any act to create, perfect, or enforce any lien against property of the estate; (5) any act to create, perfect, or enforce against property of the debtor any lien to the extent that such lien secures a claim that arose before the commencement of the case under this title; (6) any act to collect, assess, or recover a claim against the debtor that arose before the commencement of the case under this title; (7) the setoff of any debt owing to the debtor that arose before the commencement of the case under this title against any claim against the debtor; and (8) the commencement or continuation of a proceeding before the United States Tax Court concerning a tax liability of a debtor that is a corporation for a taxable period the bankruptcy court may determine or concerning the tax liability of a debtor who i
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the 60-day window for dismissal of bankruptcy proceedings, creating a contradiction with the automatic stay provision outlined in 11 U.S.C. § 362. The original contract allowed a 60-day period for dismissal or vacation of bankruptcy proceedings before termination could occur, aligning with the debtor protections offered under bankruptcy law, as indicated in the scraped snippets which detail the automatic stay. The removal of this timeframe directly conflicts with the purpose of the automatic stay, which is to provide debtors with a temporary reprieve from creditors, allowing them time for reorganization.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor shall maintain insurance for not less than the following limits and coverage with duly licensed insurance companies having an A.M. Best rating of A-, X or better. In addition to covering all of the normal operations of Vendor, this insurance shall cover all of the activities and events described under this Agreement. 9. Representations Vendor represents and warrants to Torvec the following: (i) it has the authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform hereunder in accordance with its provisions; (ii) no other person or entity has the right to be the exclusive automotive sponsor of the activities and the events set forth in this Agreement; and (iii) it will perform its obligations under this Agreement in compliance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor shall maintain insurance for not less than the following limits and coverage with insurance companies. This insurance shall cover all of the activities and events described under this Agreement. 9. Representations Vendor represents and warrants to Torvec the following: (i) it has the authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform hereunder in accordance with its provisions; (ii) no other person or entity has the right to be the exclusive automotive sponsor of the activities and the events set forth in this Agreement; and (iii) it will perform its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement that insurance companies be 'duly licensed' and have a minimum A.M. Best rating creates uncertainty. The law requires insurance companies to be licensed to operate, and specifying a rating ensures financial stability. Omitting this opens the door to using non-compliant or financially unstable insurers, potentially invalidating the coverage and violating insurance regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Insurance Law - Licensing Requirements
perturbation.law_explanation: The edited contract removes the requirement for duly licensed insurance companies with a minimum A.M. Best rating, contradicting New York Insurance Law § 1101, which mandates that insurers be licensed to conduct business in the state. Using an unlicensed insurer would render the insurance coverage invalid and expose Torvec to significant financial risk.

2025-03-28 2025-05-16 Share Facebook Email SECTION 1101 Definitions; doing an insurance business Insurance (ISC) CHAPTER 28, ARTICLE 11 § 1101. Definitions; doing an insurance business. (a) In this article: (1) "Insurance contract" means any agreement or other transaction whereby one party, the "insurer", is obligated to confer benefit of pecuniary value upon another party, the "insured" or "beneficiary", dependent upon the happening of a fortuitous event in which the insured or beneficiary has, or is expected to have at the time of such happening, a material interest which will be adversely affected by the happening of such event. (2) "Fortuitous event" means any occurrence or failure to occur which is, or is assumed by the parties to be, to a substantial extent beyond the control of either party. (3) "Contract of warranty, guaranty or suretyship" means an insurance contract only if made by a warrantor, guarantor or surety who or which, as such, is doing an insurance business. (b) (1) Except as otherwise provided in this subsection, any of the following acts in this state, effected by mail from outside this state or otherwise, by any person, firm, association, corporation or joint-stock company shall constitute doing an insurance business in this state and shall constitute doing business in the state within the meaning of section three hundred two of the civil practice law and rules: (A) making, or proposing to make, as insurer, any insurance contract, including either issuance or delivery of a policy or contract of insurance to a resident of this state or to any firm, association, or corporation authorized to do business herein, or solicitation of applications for any such policies or contracts; (B) making, or proposing to make, as warrantor, guarantor or surety, any contract of warranty, guaranty or suretyship as a vocation and not as merely incidental to any other legitimate business or activity of the warrantor, guarantor or surety; (C) collecting any premium, membership fee, assessment or other consideration for a
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the requirement for the vendor to maintain insurance with 'duly licensed insurance companies having an A.M. Best rating of A-, X or better.' This contradicts New York Insurance Law § 1101, which implies that insurers must be licensed to conduct business in the state. By removing the 'duly licensed' requirement, the modified contract potentially allows for insurance from unlicensed companies, thus creating a contract that does not adhere to state insurance regulations. The law snippet supports this, defining 'doing an insurance business' as 'making, or proposing to make, as insurer, any insurance contract, including either issuance or delivery of a policy or contract of insurance to a resident of this state...'. Using an unlicensed insurer would violate this provision.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is to be governed and construed according to the laws of the State of New York without regard to conflicts of law. The parties agree that each of them hereby submits to the jurisdiction of the New York State and federal courts for the purpose of resolving any dispute arising under this Agreement and that the exclusive venue for resolution of such disputes shall be state or federal courts located in Monroe County, New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is to be governed and construed according to principles of equity and fairness, disregarding established legal precedent. The parties agree that dispute resolution shall occur through binding arbitration in a location determined solely by Torvec.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'the laws of the State of New York' with 'principles of equity and fairness' and removing the established court system and venue clause fundamentally alters the legal framework for dispute resolution. This contradicts the principle of 'governing law', which requires adherence to a recognized legal system. Arbitrary dispute resolution locations are often deemed unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified clause, replacing governing law with 'principles of equity', directly contradicts the established legal precedent codified in 9 U.S.C. § 10, specifically regarding venue for arbitration. By allowing Torvec to unilaterally determine the arbitration location, it potentially deprives the other party of a fair and impartial hearing, as the location may be chosen to their disadvantage, making it difficult to challenge the arbitrator's decision.

Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration- (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon s
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 ; Pub. L. 101–552, § 5 , Nov. 15, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2745 ; Pub. L. 102–354, § 5(b)(4) , Aug. 26, 1992 , 106 Stat. 946 ; Pub. L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by replacing 'the laws of the State of New York' with 'principles of equity and fairness' and allowing Torvec to unilaterally determine the arbitration location, which deviates from standard legal procedure and potentially deprives the other party of a fair hearing. 9 U.S.C. § 10 addresses the grounds for vacating an arbitration award, indicating a structure and process rooted in legal standards, which the changed text undermines. By allowing a single party to select the arbitration venue, the modified text could lead to unfair or biased outcomes, potentially warranting the vacation of the award under 9 U.S.C. § 10. Snippet 2 mentions the ability of a court to vacate an award where arbitrators exceeded their powers or acted improperly, and the unilateral determination of venue could be construed as exceeding powers or acting with evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, as mentioned in 9 U.S.C. § 10(a)(2).
